item.title: Samsung and SK Hynix plan $600bn chipmaking expansion
item.description: Companies to invest alongside South Korean government in factories to meet surging demand for memory chips

item.title: UK’s Bridgepoint buys real estate unit in $1.4bn bet on US property
item.description: Private capital group is looking beyond its traditional speciality in corporate buyouts

item.title: China targets more Japanese companies with export controls
item.description: Further restrictions on sales of ‘dual-use’ items with both civilian and military purposes

item.title: BT and Verizon to create new international business in $625mn deal
item.description: Joint venture will be milestone in UK group’s strategy of refocusing on its domestic operations

item.title: AInflation is real but tiny
item.description: Plus, European stocks ain’t cheap

item.title: Artificial intelligence and Engels’ Pause
item.description: Politics could be a bigger constraint than power and compute

item.title: European producers press Brussels to shut Russian aluminium loophole
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Pharma sector push EU to close life sciences funding gap

item.title: How Citadel became an energy giant
item.description: Plus, Venezuela’s huge debt pile, Japanese IPO slump and Constantin Brâncuşi in Berlin

item.title: Warsh’s Fed communication is adding volatility to rates markets
item.description: The downsides of seeking more fundamental information from markets will be in greater surprises

item.title: How Vinícius Júnior became central to Brazil’s World Cup dream
item.description: Real Madrid winger holds key to Brazil’s hopes of winning first world title since 2002

item.title: Europe risks starting winter with gas stocks at 15-year low
item.description: Storage facilities in the EU are not being refilled fast enough ahead of colder months

item.title: Virgin Media O2 bonds slide as broadband rivals squeeze cash flow
item.description: Telecoms group’s bonds tumble as altnets take market share and investors fret over £2bn Netomnia deal

item.title: Freight shipping costs surge as companies race to beat new Trump tariffs
item.description: Rates reach highest since 2024 Red Sea crisis in anticipation of fresh levies from US

item.title: Stray Ukraine drones worth the price of hitting Russia, says Estonian minister
item.description: Margus Tsahkna asserts that EU negotiations with Moscow are premature

item.title: The new AI-based world order
item.description: A single factor is now dictating the hierarchy of global returns

item.title: Volkswagen’s brutal jobs cull sparks prospect of sale of crown jewels
item.description: Manufacturer squeezed €10bn valuation from cloak-and-dagger Everllence auction but may need more to fund restructuring

item.title: The leap of faith behind SpaceX’s mega bond deal
item.description: The views of equity and bond investors might one day be at odds with each other

item.title: Can the IT sector solve the UK’s growth problem?
item.description: The industry is expanding far faster than finance. Regions outside London want a piece of the pie

item.title: ‘You saw it get out of hand’: WeWork’s CEO on rebuilding a wayward brand
item.description: After half a century at a traditional property group, John Santora is helping the one-time disrupter grow up

item.title: AI fuels record $200bn M&A boom in US power sector
item.description: Companies in dealmaking blitz as they seek to build the energy infrastructure for data centres

item.title: Battery start-ups turn to spare Asian factories to avoid Northvolt fate
item.description: Western outsourcing to build scale risks China’s increased control of critical supply chains, analysts warn

item.title: Make your garden a home for hedgehogs
item.description: Five gadgets for the greater outdoors

item.title: AI wakes up the sleepy US power sector
item.description: Companies in dealmaking blitz as they seek to build the energy infrastructure for data centres

item.title: Japan IPOs fall to 15-year low with no rapid rebound in sight
item.description: Tokyo’s surging stocks fail to jump-start lacklustre listings market in first half of this year

item.title: Sovereign funds move from public markets to private to ride AI wave
item.description: High concentration in stock markets and national security concerns send SWFs to private credit and infrastructure

item.title: FirstFT: AI ‘exuberance’ threatens the global economy, BIS warns
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Aramco helicopter crashes at Saudi port, and Indian snack giant Haldiram’s preparing European expansion

item.title: Airlines brace for up to $127bn in extra costs from carbon credit shortage
item.description: Emirates could have highest expense because of reliance on long-haul flights, MSCI Carbon Markets says

item.title: Heathrow’s £33bn expansion is ‘like a luxury Mercedes’, says airline group boss
item.description: Head of Star Alliance warns of ‘eye-watering’ costs of third runway project

item.title: Aramco helicopter crashes at Saudi port
item.description: At least 14 killed in Ras Tanura incident just hours after US-Iran strikes threaten fragile deal to reopen Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Robots, not chatbots, will realise AI’s potential
item.description: Factory-floor applications of the technology could significantly enhance rich-world economies
